story.title: Exploring All About Me
story.text:
When Ms Ahmed announced that Term project would focus on all about me, a small murmur ran across the classroom. Some students smiled; others frowned. Kavya thought about it for a long moment before opening her notebook to a clean page. She had learned this year that the best projects begin with good questions rather than quick answers, so she wrote three at the top of her page.

"Your first task," said Ms Ahmed, "is to research three interesting facts about all about me. But I want more than facts. I want you to think about why those facts matter. Choose one fact and explain the deeper reason it is important." She wrote the word 'significance' on the whiteboard and underlined it twice. Then she added, "Remember, information becomes knowledge only when you make sense of it."

After lunch, the class split into small groups. Kavya joined Ethan and Zara. They found a stack of books, opened three tabs on the class laptop, and began taking notes. Zara suggested they draw a mind map. Ethan liked to make lists. Kavya preferred to sketch diagrams. By using all three ways together, they gathered a huge amount of information in one hour.

When it was time to share, Kavya volunteered to speak first. She stood up and pointed to her diagram, explaining not just the facts, but why she thought each one mattered. Ms Ahmed nodded slowly.

"That," she said, "is what learning sounds like. Not just facts. Questions, ideas, and reasons." The class clapped. Kavya sat down, cheeks warm, and turned to the next page of her notebook. She had a feeling that this project would teach her more than any lesson had all year.

How did each group member prefer to organise information?
Why does Ms Ahmed say information becomes knowledge?
options: When you make sense of it

How did the three students combine their strengths?
What did Ms Ahmed say about facts versus questions?

story.title: Exploring Farms and Food Chains
story.text:
When Ms Ahmed announced that Term project would focus on farms and food chains, a small murmur ran across the classroom. Some students smiled; others frowned. Kavya thought about it for a long moment before opening her notebook to a clean page. She had learned this year that the best projects begin with good questions rather than quick answers, so she wrote three at the top of her page.

"Your first task," said Ms Ahmed, "is to research three interesting facts about farms and food chains. But I want more than facts. I want you to think about why those facts matter. Choose one fact and explain the deeper reason it is important." She wrote the word 'significance' on the whiteboard and underlined it twice. Then she added, "Remember, information becomes knowledge only when you make sense of it."

After lunch, the class split into small groups. Kavya joined Ethan and Zara. They found a stack of books, opened three tabs on the class laptop, and began taking notes. Zara suggested they draw a mind map. Ethan liked to make lists. Kavya preferred to sketch diagrams. By using all three ways together, they gathered a huge amount of information in one hour.

When it was time to share, Kavya volunteered to speak first. She stood up and pointed to her diagram, explaining not just the facts, but why she thought each one mattered. Ms Ahmed nodded slowly.

"That," she said, "is what learning sounds like. Not just facts. Questions, ideas, and reasons." The class clapped. Kavya sat down, cheeks warm, and turned to the next page of her notebook. She had a feeling that this project would teach her more than any lesson had all year.

mcq.q: What was the class's Term project topic?
What word did Ms Ahmed underline twice?
How did each group member prefer to organise information?
Why does Ms Ahmed say information becomes knowledge?
options: When you make sense of it

short: Why did Kavya start with questions instead of answers?
How did the three students combine their strengths?
What did Ms Ahmed say about facts versus questions?
inference: What does Ms Ahmed value most in learning? Use one example.
Why do you think Kavya feels this project will teach her more than any other lesson?

story.title: Exploring Transport Time

story.title: Exploring Under the Microscope
story.text:
When Ms Ahmed announced that Term project would focus on under the microscope, a small murmur ran across the classroom. Some students smiled; others frowned. Kavya thought about it for a long moment before opening her notebook to a clean page. She had learned this year that the best projects begin with good questions rather than quick answers, so she wrote three at the top of her page.

"Your first task," said Ms Ahmed, "is to research three interesting facts about under the microscope. But I want more than facts. I want you to think about why those facts matter. Choose one fact and explain the deeper reason it is important." She wrote the word 'significance' on the whiteboard and underlined it twice. Then she added, "Remember, information becomes knowledge only when you make sense of it."

After lunch, the class split into small groups. Kavya joined Ethan and Zara. They found a stack of books, opened three tabs on the class laptop, and began taking notes. story.title: Exploring Dinosaurs & Fossils
story.text:
When Ms Ahmed announced that Term project would focus on dinosaurs & fossils, a small murmur ran across the classroom. Some students smiled; others frowned. Kavya thought about it for a long moment before opening her notebook to a clean page. She had learned this year that the best projects begin with good questions rather than quick answers, so she wrote three at the top of her page.

"Your first task," said Ms Ahmed, "is to research three interesting facts about dinosaurs & fossils. But I want more than facts. I want you to think about why those facts matter. Choose one fact and explain the deeper reason it is important." She wrote the word 'significance' on the whiteboard and underlined it twice. Then she added, "Remember, information becomes knowledge only when you make sense of it."

After lunch, the class split into small groups. Kavya joined Ethan and Zara. They found a stack of books, opened three tabs on the class laptop, and began taking notes. Zara suggested they draw a mind map. Ethan liked to make lists. Kavya preferred to sketch diagrams. By using all three ways together, they gathered a huge amount of information in one hour.

When it was time to share, Kavya volunteered to speak first. She stood up and pointed to her diagram, explaining not just the facts, but why she thought each one mattered. Ms Ahmed nodded slowly.

"That," she said, "is what learning sounds like. Not just facts. Questions, ideas, and reasons." The class clapped. Kavya sat down, cheeks warm, and turned to the next page of her notebook. She had a feeling that this project would teach her more than any lesson had all year.

story.title: Exploring Leaders & Change-Makers
story.text:
When Ms Ahmed announced that Term project would focus on leaders & change-makers, a small murmur ran across the classroom. Some students smiled; others frowned. Kavya thought about it for a long moment before opening her notebook to a clean page. She had learned this year that the best projects begin with good questions rather than quick answers, so she wrote three at the top of her page.

"Your first task," said Ms Ahmed, "is to research three interesting facts about leaders & change-makers. But I want more than facts. I want you to think about why those facts matter. Choose one fact and explain the deeper reason it is important." She wrote the word 'significance' on the whiteboard and underlined it twice. Then she added, "Remember, information becomes knowledge only when you make sense of it."

After lunch, the class split into small groups. Kavya joined Ethan and Zara. They found a stack of books, opened three tabs on the class laptop, and began taking notes. Zara suggested they draw a mind map. Ethan liked to make lists. Kavya preferred to sketch diagrams. By using all three ways together, they gathered a huge amount of information in one hour.

When it was time to share, Kavya volunteered to speak first. She stood up and pointed to her diagram, explaining not just the facts, but why she thought each one mattered. Ms Ahmed nodded slowly.

"That," she said, "is what learning sounds like. Not just facts. Questions, ideas, and reasons." The class clapped. Kavya sat down, cheeks warm, and turned to the next page of her notebook. She had a feeling that this project would teach her more than any lesson had all year.

story.title: Exploring Global Festivals
story.text:
When Ms Ahmed announced that Term project would focus on global festivals, a small murmur ran across the classroom. Some students smiled; others frowned. Kavya thought about it for a long moment before opening her notebook to a clean page. She had learned this year that the best projects begin with good questions rather than quick answers, so she wrote three at the top of her page.

"Your first task," said Ms Ahmed, "is to research three interesting facts about global festivals. But I want more than facts. I want you to think about why those facts matter. Choose one fact and explain the deeper reason it is important." She wrote the word 'significance' on the whiteboard and underlined it twice. Then she added, "Remember, information becomes knowledge only when you make sense of it."

After lunch, the class split into small groups. Kavya joined Ethan and Zara. They found a stack of books, opened three tabs on the class laptop, and began taking notes. Zara suggested they draw a mind map. Ethan liked to make lists. Kavya preferred to sketch diagrams. By using all three ways together, they gathered a huge amount of information in one hour.

When it was time to share, Kavya volunteered to speak first. She stood up and pointed to her diagram, explaining not just the facts, but why she thought each one mattered. Ms Ahmed nodded slowly.

"That," she said, "is what learning sounds like. Not just facts. Questions, ideas, and reasons." The class clapped. Kavya sat down, cheeks warm, and turned to the next page of her notebook. She had a feeling that this project would teach her more than any lesson had all year.
